speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us that the war had come to an end, you'd got a telegram from your Czech friends to say that you had nobody left alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got a letter from your sister in Israel saying--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't go back--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Try and go to Israel with all the other refugees.

speaker: interviewee
text: All-- all people go-- go [? maybe ?] people don't go, nowhere. And then, we emigrated from Russia to-- to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you move to Poland? It's a long way.

speaker: interviewee
text: They did give us train. They gave us trains. Was arranged, you know? Cattle trains. But you had to-- we-- we went maybe six weeks, or more than six weeks, in these trains. You had to prepare bread, you had to prepare everything, and to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: For a six weeks' journey on a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: On a train. It was 1,500 people, more, in this train. Very big trains. And we did go, also. We did stay three days, we go three days. We-- we came to Poland. We came to-- to Poland. And we went to Zarow, a small place in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Zarow?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is Z-A-R-O-- Zarow. This, Zarow, was belong to Germany in-- in the good days. But when Poland took it back, they took a few-- a few towns from them. We came to Zarow. And we did get hold of a place where to-- to be, where to settle down a little bit. But I couldn't be there. Everything it smell with blood for me. I couldn't eat, I couldn't drink, I couldn't-- wherever I did move, I did feel as I will not survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then it was the organization, the-- the Zionist movement, straight away. They organized us. And they told us this story, as we will have to go a very difficult time. To go, maybe by train, maybe not but train, maybe by foot. We will take you out. We will take you out. They took it out to Germany to DP camps. You see? To go to the DP camps was a very big thing to go through. Because the Russian people did, why did you leave Russia? Why did you leave? Where are you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did-- one thing was-- was told to us. Not-- you don't understand nothing. You don't know where you belong. You don't know where you going. You don't know nothing. You-- when they ask you, you don't know anything. You can't tell them anything you don't know. We going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would Russia not have allowed you out, otherwise?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You see, why-- you had to tell the reason why you left Russia all of a sudden. My-- my-- my argument went, as I go to my town. I want to go home. I want see-- to find somebody. But that was a lie. I didn't go home. I did go with these people. And we went from the Ihud. That is the Zionist movement did arrange to go. We went by train to Bratislava-- to-- that in Poland. I forgot to tell you the story. When I came to Poland, I was in Kielce, near-- near Lodz. It was already-- they-- they did say, they didn't know as one Jew did left. The thought all the Jewish is already kaput. Nobody's left. When they saw the trains they come in the immigrants, it was a terrible thing, like a revolution, Kielce. And this time, I knew that we must quick move from Poland, because it's very bad. I still went to Lodz. I went to a Lodz, I saw--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they so upset to see the alive Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, terrible. They used to storm the-- the-- the trains-- to storm-- storm the trains. And we went to--

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Because Hitler did tell-- tell them, not one Jew will be left. That what how he took all this thing. When he walked in, in any place, then he did say, I'm not-- I don't want your place. I want you to make free from the Jewish people. That was his-- So we went still too Lodz-- I went to see the ghetto, and I went in a few places. I had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why anything left there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Not in the ghetto. The-- the town was wonderful. Lodz was wonderful. And I took a lot of-- from-- from Uzbekistan. I did carry a lot of dry fruit-- bags of dry fruit, like that, with me. And I passed by a very big shop, in Lodz. And I came in-- I don't know what makes me-- to ask him if he would buy, from me, the fruit. Because I haven't got any money. That's finished. Everything's finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he start asking me questions, from where I am, and what that is. And what-- I-- I didn't know, must I tell him, or mustn't I tell him. I don't know. He's a Polish man. It's an older man. And I say, what can I lose? I can lose nothing. I tell him, is I am from Dubno. He say, from which part of Dubno? Is something-- is-- something I see in your face, as I saw you some, there is. And I'm so scared, I'm absolutely there.

speaker: interviewee
text: He say he was in the Polish army, in Dubno. And he used to come to our shop. He took from me the fruit. He paid me the money. He asked me if I would like to come and be with them. It will help me. I say, no. I didn't trust anybody. I don't go nowhere. I have to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where will you sleep? I say, I've got-- That was 63 [? Gdanska, ?] remember, still now, where my friend's sister survived, in this place. And she went with us from Namangan to here. And she say, wherever, if you will be in Lodz find me. You will find me there. And everybody did split in other places. So I went, and I find her there.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a few days, and I had address to another-- to my friend's. What I used to sewing with her. She did also came to Lodz. And I had the address where she stays. I came up, and was there, three or four women were they been through Auschwitz. And they was absolutely mad. You know, these day, they still wasn't settled. They was absolutely not normal, you know, from all this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I say to her, is so and so here? She says, she went away to Bialystok, to see if somebody is left from her family. But, she told me, as you will come. And I can let you to sleep over. Me and my friends. We came in middle of the night. We want to go away. We was so scared of these three women. They was absolutely mad. You know? They was-- not-- they couldn't control themselves. And she came up, and she said, don't be scared. We will not do nothing. I say, no, I can't sleep. I'm not scared, nothing. That is-- was the last night. And then, I went to see my-- if my husband's brother-in-law. You didn't say-- tell me you had got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: I will tell you now. I was married. When I left Namangan, I was married. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you get married to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- to my [? Wolf ?] Glezer. And he was from Vilna. He traveled with me the whole time. But as a man, he couldn't go where I could go. You know? Where a woman could go in, he couldn't go in. So I was always going with my friends. With these--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he part of your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was part of my group. Yes. So he says to me--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you marry him?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we left Namangan. He says to me, I know, as my brother-in-law is in Lodz. Ask in the Jewish committee-- you know, you could always get to know-- Jewish people was so on the fingers. You can count on the fingers as you could find.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came in. And I say, so and so, is it [? die ?] or-- oh yes, he's here and here. Are you want to get married? I say, I don't want to get married. He's such a nice man. I said, let me only give me the address. Let me find him. And I find him. And I took him to Zarov for a few days. That are left-- Was that your husband's brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband's brother-- not my husband-- my husband's sister's husband. Not my-- he-- he went away from Vilna together with my husband. Straight away. He went away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how he did come? Because, through a doctor, the doctor saved him. And he went. And he was in Lodz. I took him for a few days, he wanted, in Zarov. And then he start going. Then to start going there. Who is it? The group of 10 people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not 10. It was already 500 people. The organization took us.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, these are a group of people who are trying to get into Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not trying. That-- that-- that was organized from Israel. That was the-- the Haganah did organize, how you could go. You know, here is a boat. Here is a boat. You haven't got a passport. You haven't got nothing. Nobody wants to let you through.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a terrible thing to go through from Poland to Bratislava. You know? We went by train, we stopped, and then we have to walk. The-- the-- the boat has to go through. They did shake you, you know like? To see, maybe, you are espionage or something. Where are you going? Why are you going? And we had not to understand them. We had not to answer questions. It was a terrible things. In the daytime, we used to hide ourselves. In the nights we used to walk. So we went from Bratislava. We had a train to-- to Austria. When we went to Austria. The American army surrounded us. They-- they surrounded us. And we were six weeks in tents-- in army tents-- and filled with five gram.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, the American people-- the American soldiers eat very thin breads. They don't even eat, you know. The bread is very thin. They used to give us a few slices of bread, and some other things. And, for us, the few slices is what-- just like nothing. And we were six weeks in this.

speaker: interviewer
text: First of all, they was very scared that we will bring a sickness. You know? They used to spray as with such a lot of spray that it was impossible to believe. Then I got sick, me. And they took me by ambulance to the hospital. I came to the hospital. And I know the group will go further. Because they have to go. They-- they will not stay in this. What must I do? They don't know where I am-- the group doesn't know where I am.

speaker: interviewee
text: They asked me this German. I understood every word that he speaks to me. And I make myself stupid-- I don't know nothing. He say, I can help you, if you will explain to me. I said, no, I don't go any explanations. Nothing. 5 o'clock in the next day, in the morning-- two days. I was two days. I had a bath, I wash myself. You know, in hospital you have hot water, and a towel everything good. I said, no. I have to run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how will I get my way? I don't-- I went by ambulance. And I did ask people where to go. And I was maybe three days, and then we moved by trains. So you eventually find your way back to the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I find-- it was very difficult. It was not-- I say in three minutes. Believe me, my heart was under my feet. I didn't know where I'm going.

speaker: interviewer
text: You must have been very weak, also, if you were sick enough to be admitted to a hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was. You see, I had infection in my throat. And then-- the army-- the-- the American army don't play with you, because you can give a sickness to the others. Straight away, they isolate you. There's not such a thing. And then, we went to-- from Bratislava, we went to-- to-- to the-- not the [INAUDIBLE] but to Wetzlar-- to the DP camps. It was 1,500 people. We went to the DP camps. It was five in a room, accommodated. That was the-- the places where the German army used to stay. That was already-- and, absolutely, you have a little bit of water. You've got where to sleep. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Truly, I'm sure it was the nicest place you'd stayed in since 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And the best-- the best thing we find, a barrel from herring, so we can wash ourselves. We went into the barrels and spill the water. You know, a little bit of hot water, to wash ourselves. That was a joke. But it's not a joke. That was, absolutely, something that you didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a herring barrel, that you used to wash yourself in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We find it. You know, the-- in the-- in Germany and Poland, is where it runs the water from the rain. You collect the water in a barrel-- empty barrel. Because it's soft water. And these days wasn't things luxury to wash, or change, or something. But with this water was soft. So we-- we steal this barrel from the-- and he had washed ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was already in Wetzlar. Then they meet-- the United-- Your husband was there also?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband was also with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband, and my friends. Four of us, we didn't-- never move without-- we didn't move. We going, you going. We going, you going. You know? We came to-- to Wetzlar. They need people to work for the-- You know, the United Nations, it was there. They did send delegates from every country. And their-- their quarters was in Wetzlar, in town. Not in where we did stay. They need two people to work, to-- to the food, and to see. She heard my girlfriend, stays in the other side to me. They took me and her. And we start working every day. We had-- we couldn't sleep there. But we came in the morning. We had already ready food. We had everything, luxury. That was a luxury job, very luxurious. And then, I met, from the Joint, you know, Dr. [? Kaplan. ?] He was a Jewish man from New York, but actually was born in Poland. He was born in Walcz. And he used-- him and was [? Laura ?] [? Weinberg-- ?] too from America. That was from every country-- delegates from every country.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I did work very nicely. And-- and they start explaining to me, Dr. [? Kaplan ?] say, I know you want to go to Israel, and you want to go. But you suffered such a lot. You will-- you don't know what the state of Israel will be. Still wasn't declared, you know? You don't know what will be there. Let's make papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, I'm not going nowhere. Don't-- don't speak to me, because I will not go. I don't want to go. I haven't got-- first of all, I want to go to my sister, to-- who is left from my cousins, still. I don't want to go nowhere. That's my place, and I'm going. He used to speak to me, day and night, but nothing did help. I didn't-- they did tell us, is they will come. You know, it used to be three families they taking to this and this in this country. And we received letters. They gave them homes. They gave them everything. And they did really look after these people when they got-- I said, I'm not going. I don't-- I'm not used to luxury. And I will go where I-- where I want to go. And they used to tell us from the Haganah-- don't register, don't tell them your name, nothing. When they come, don't register, don't say, you don't know. And that was like that, because who did register had to go. Then start the [INAUDIBLE], you know, to go. And, in Wetzlar, I used to smuggle a lot for the Haganah. You know, there is the biggest factory from like the-- the-- the binoculars, and everything, in Wetzlar-- one of the biggest factories. We used to buy for coffee. For coffee, you could buy something. You know, we used to buy. Because it was illegal. We used to buy a lot for the Haganah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Haganah used to go to the [INAUDIBLE] factory and buy binoculars with coffee?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to buy, not from the factory. They wouldn't-- you know some people, but they need it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You buy it from the workers?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the workers. They used to give us. And you pack a suitcase like that, and you going to Munich with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they wanted to try and get into Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Palestine. They take-- there was already easy. When was already in Munich, they could-- they could ship it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to smuggle the binoculars?

speaker: interviewee
text: We smuggled. We used to put it onto the-- somewhere under the seat, and sit there. I don't know to whom belongs. If they will open, they will open. If they will take away, they will take away. It was a risk. But once a week, I used to go with my husband. And once a week used to go my other friend. And we did-- we met a lot of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you'd sit on the train. You'd pretend the suitcase didn't belong to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but go through with the suitcases, still police also working. You know? It still-- it wasn't an easy-- an easy thing to do. But I want to do it. I knew I-- if I risk my life, I risk my life. I didn't have children, nothing. So the life was so cheap, as you don't think what will happen. That was twice a week. And I met a lot of people from Israel and from-- a lot of people. I met one, what I met her after that in Israel. She was actually run in Germany. I met a lot-- a lot of people there. Then-- then we were in Wetzlar still, '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where would you take it to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Munich. That was-- that was a special place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would people there meet you and take it from you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We went in. We used to have lunch there. And was-- is like your-- the Zionist Federation works. [? They did work there. ?] [DRINKS] Then we start to-- who will go with the [INAUDIBLE]. It wasn't a easy thing to go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We did register. Because I did work in this [INAUDIBLE], I did get paid. So I bought a lot of things for Israel-- prepared myself. I bought pots, and I bought, everything what you need for a kitchen sink, you know. It was a lot to-- to take. A lot of things if you only had money. One thing that I forgot to tell you, when I came to Wetzlar, and I did see in the window, was the soap, from Juden fats. The soap made from Juden fats.

speaker: interviewer
text: | Was the window?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wetzlar. When we came in to Wetzlar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it in a shop window?

speaker: interviewee
text: The shop window.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fat-- soap made out of Jewish fat on sale in a window in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the windows. They still didn't manage to-- to clear it up. And that was-- that was the good one, things already. They used to surround our-- you know, the camps-- the DP camps. They used to surround with wire. Three times a week. Something did happen, straight away. Straight away, they used to. We couldn't get out, we couldn't get in. But that was nothing to compare with all the-- the difficulties, what I went through. That was really like to eat the ice cream, is nothing, to-- to compare what I went through. Then when Israel was declared, we had a very big-- not a party, all the 1,500 people was congregated in a-- in a place, and with speakers from Israel, with speakers from all over [? there. ?] A lot of people came. And one thing what they did ask us, don't go to any country. We need you. We need you people. We need you.

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot of people couldn't wait, you know? You-- you was two, three families in one room. That was already a luxury, but still. You know, it was-- some people with small children they really couldn't-- couldn't do it. So did register, and my friend, and my husband, and other friend. So this is now 1948?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was 1946, end of 1946. '46 '47-- '48. The end of November. We left Wetzlar November '47. We came to Israel in-- in December. We came in December. Because we was hiding in Marseilles for three solid weeks, because illegal-- we must go illegal. 40 people, in a house like that. 40 people was in a-- in a-- in a house, like that, for three solid weeks. Still came there-- the-- the boat from, [? was it, ?] Israel, already-- the [? Negbah. ?] The [? Negbah ?] could accommodate maybe 200 people or it was maybe 500, 600 people. It was lined like herrings in this-- in this boat. We waited three solid weeks in Marseilles.

speaker: interviewer
text: If Israel had already been declared--

speaker: interviewee
text: But the English people didn't let you in. Oh, they didn't let you in. You couldn't just go. It was like water. You-- you know, like to America, you have you have to-- they let in so and so many people, not much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was the [? Negbah ?] an illegal boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was-- the people were--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the state of Israel hadn't been declared officially?

speaker: interviewee
text: Declared-- it was declared. But English-- the English people didn't let still in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Israel was declared in May 1948.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight, yes. When I came-- I will just tell. When I came to Israel, when I went out, was still, when we came to--

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you went out in December '47, then?

speaker: interviewer
text: But in December, '48, it was not-- Israel had now allowed immigration.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was still not allowed, immigration. We still couldn't illegal. We couldn't go. When I came to Israel, and a week later was opened the parliament. I remember this speech, I remember the-- this thing and-- and Ben Yehuda street, I had to walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came to Israel. It was also difficult. We came after two weeks. And we came in the boat. We-- to go with the [? Negbah. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Was from-- from the Jewish woman from Paris, from Europe, from France-- they gave us chocolates, and parcels for everybody, going on the-- I was all sick on the boat, as I didn't know where I'm going, and what I'm going. When we came to Israel, we came to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any difficulty landing in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not difficult. Not, not difficult. But again was-- in-- the Jewish people want to know what you brought, because they were so scared. You know? Some people came-- they don't know you, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: We came to Israel in-- in the night. They ask me where I want to go? Which-- because the Ma'abarot was in all places. I thought as Netanya is near to Rishon LeZion where my sister is. That what I did think. I didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I went to Netanya. And we went in the night. We came to the Ma'abarot. You know, the-- it was maybe, 400, or 500, to 600 immigrants. And we went there yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they used to give us food. They used to give us everything, because we didn't have anything. And I came to-- I came on Thursday or Wednesday night. [INAUDIBLE] I must go and see my sister. How will I go?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, the last letter from Wetzlar. I told you not to wait for letters, because I don't know where I will be. Don't worry. Soon I will come. And my sister did, in every Ma'abarot, there was the-- the Israeli, you know the Israeli [INAUDIBLE]. And she used you say, if so and so will come, let me know. But-- but it was-- I-- I didn't let her know. And Friday I decided I'm going to my sister. I'm going. I had $10 with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I find it-- I find it-- where this-- where the buses stays. And I went, and I ask-- I ask him in Yiddish. I say to him, would you like to take me to this and this place? I have only $10. He laughed at me. He said, you don't need it. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he took me to Rishon LeZion. And that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took you for nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: For nothing. And [INAUDIBLE] is a street. [INAUDIBLE] is the [INAUDIBLE] from our man. But he did give the name of it, he said, here is [INAUDIBLE] [? Dror, ?] where you must go down, and that is [INAUDIBLE]. And you will find your sister. I went. I came in. She collapsed. She really couldn't-- she couldn't-- she didn't believe. And I was looking good. I had a rest on the-- on the ship, already-- in the boat. I had food. I was prepared myself. I had a pair of flannel trousers. And I had a little jersey. I was looking good, because I-- I had already what to eat, and picked up weight-- from 42 kilos, I weighed already 50. That was different. And then I spent with her, the-- the Saturday. It was still the blackout-- we couldn't have the-- the lights. Still in Israel, was still the fighting, when I came. It was still with no lights, and you had to-- the bombs still did go. The Arabs they didn't let us live. And I was staying only Friday, Friday night, Saturday and Sunday. And there, I met my cousin. But he was also in Israel, afterwards. He died only a year ago. Next door to me, we did stay like this door and that. And he survived. First of all, he was in the Russian army. He was mobilized in the Russian army. And then he was mobilized in the Israeli army, because he-- he knew how to provide the tank. He was shot down in the-- in this-- this part of the hand, having two weeks, a week, free. So he came to see me. And, you know that, it was joy and cry had and everything. [DRINKS] Then I left. I went to the Ma'barot back. I left the Ma'barot. We sold everything. Well, not everything-- the most what was brought from Germany, to get a room in Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewer
text: We didn't want-- we did see us, in the Ma'barot, such a lot of people. And it's people with children, and it's-- and is going on. I don't know what to tell you, what's going on. Not-- not hungry. It was food. It was-- it was where to put you in tents. We was in tents, there. And six, seven people in one tent. You know? This was also not an easy thing to go through. So we sold. I did ask in the letters, what must I bring to Israel to get some money, because we couldn't have any money. We didn't belong nowhere. So I had mementos. I said things. I had teddy bears, what I did sell.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we did hire one room, under [INAUDIBLE]. One room without a kitchen, nothing. She used to tell us, as you can use in-- in the bathroom, you can-- we had a little stove from-- electric stove. We bought it in Germany. We have two-- two-- like that big. You can put it on, in the bathroom. And we will not have it, but you can use it. Anyway.
